entry.title: Freight Order Quantity Analysis
entry.content:
In SAP Transportation Management in transportation execution and operational visibility scenarios. In one weekly review, the business team wanted to understand whether transportation volume was evenly distributed across carriers.

The first comparison was based mainly on the number of completed Freight Orders. At first glance, two carriers looked almost equal because their Freight Order counts were close. However, when we reviewed the transported gross weight and gross volume, we found that the actual workload was not the same.

For confidentiality, I have used representative carrier names and adjusted figures. The scenario is based on the type of operational review I have worked on, but the numbers are not production values.
Accessing the App the Freight Order Quantity Analysis app can be accessed from the SAP Fiori launchpad.

  1. Open the SAP Fiori launchpad.
  2. Search for Freight Order Quantity Analysis.
  3. Open the app with Fiori app ID F2676.
  4. From the overview page, select the relevant analytical card or data point.
  5. Navigate to the detailed view to analyze the Freight Orders behind the selected KPI.

The overview page provides different analytical cards, such as gross weight, gross volume, quantity distribution, carrier quantity analysis, shipper quantity analysis, ship to party quantity analysis, transportation mode and utilization.


Figure 1: Freight Order Quantity Analysis overview page - Fiori app F2676

Filters Used for the Analysis from the overview page, I selected the Carrier Quantity Analysis card and opened the detailed view. For the analysis, I used the following filter and display selections:

  • Transportation Mode Category: Road
  • Destination Country/Region: United States
  • View By: Carrier
  • Measure: Gross Weight
  • Display Weight Unit: Metric Tons
  • Display Volume Unit: Cubic Metres
  • Display Quantity Unit: Each
  • Completion Period: Selected completion weeks within the available current and previous month period

The standard app focuses on completed Freight Orders for the current and previous month. Because of this, I used the Completion Year/Week field to compare weekly movement trends.

These fields helped to compare the number of Freight Orders against the actual transportation volume.


Figure 2: Detailed view with carrier, transportation mode, units and completion week filters

Representative Carrier Comparison, The original business question was simple: Which carrier handled more work this week?
If we looked only at the number of Freight Orders, Carrier A appeared to have handled slightly more work than Carrier B.

Measure

Carrier A

Carrier B

Completed Freight Orders

42

39

Gross Weight

840 metric tons

1,260 metric tons

Gross Volume

1,960 m³

2,450 m³

Based on Freight Order count alone, Carrier A looked slightly higher because it completed 42 Freight Orders compared with Carrier B’s 39 Freight Orders. However, the quantity analysis showed a different story. Carrier B completed three fewer Freight Orders, but transported approximately 50% more gross weight. Carrier B also transported higher gross volume.
This finding changed the discussion. Carrier A had more documents, but Carrier B had a higher actual transportation workload. Carrier B was handling fewer but heavier movements, while Carrier A was handling a larger number of smaller shipments.

The main finding was that Freight Order count should not be used as the only measure of carrier workload. In this example, the business initially assumed that Carrier A had a slightly higher workload because it had more completed Freight Orders. After reviewing gross weight and gross volume, we could see that Carrier B had consumed more transportation capacity.
This was useful for three reasons:
1. It gave the team a better view of actual carrier workload.
2. It supported a more meaningful capacity discussion with carriers.
3. It helped explain why carrier utilization may look different from the document count.

The app also helped identify weekly movement patterns. When I changed the view to Completion Week, I could see the week in which Carrier B’s transported weight increased. This made it easier to review the underlying Freight Orders and understand what caused the change. Drilling Down into the Freight Orders after identifying the increase for Carrier B, I reviewed the detailed table and checked the Freight Orders behind the result.
Compared the following information:

  • Source and destination locations
  • Gross weight and volume
  • Number of containers
  • Transportation mode
  • Freight Order completion date
  • Carrier assignment

The list showed that Carrier B had handled several high weight movements to the same destination region during that week. This explained why the gross weight was higher even though the Freight Order count was lower. This is the part I found most useful. The overview helped identify the trend and the detailed view helped explain the reason behind it.


Figure 3: Carrier comparison showing Freight Order count, gross weight, and gross volume

It is also important to understand that this analysis is based on completed Freight Orders. Freight Orders that are still being planned or executed may not be represented in the same way. This can explain differences between the application and another operational report that includes planned or in execution documents.
From a technical perspective, the app uses the C_FrtOrdQuantityQuery CDS view. The C_FRTORDQUANTITYQUERY_CDS OData service must be activated in the SAP Gateway system. Users also need the required SAP Transportation Management and SAP Fiori authorizations to access the app and view the analytical data. No custom development was required for this analysis. The key requirements were correct Freight Order data, the required authorizations and activation of the supporting analytical service.

Conclusion
The Freight Order Quantity Analysis app gives better insight into transportation volumes and supports more informed decision making. It helps teams understand not just how many Freight Orders are moving, but also how much business volume is being executed. The app may look simple at first, but when used together with operational knowledge, it can provide useful insights that are not always visible from standard Freight Order reports.

Reference: https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_ON-PREMISE/e3dc5400c1cc41d1bc0ae0e7fd9aa5a2/612fbe25aed64068a7aca8e8e8339de1.html?utm_source=chatgpt.com

entry.title: Connecting SAP Commerce Cloud to SAP Datasphere Using SQL View Gateway
entry.content:
Connecting SAP Commerce Cloud to SAP Datasphere Using SQL View Gateway

This is the third and final post in a series on SQL View Gateway. The first post introduced SQL View Gateway and explained why it exists. The second post covered end-to-end configuration. This post shows how to connect SQL View Gateway to SAP Datasphere — and what to do with the data once it arrives there.

SAP Datasphere can consume Commerce data from SQL View Gateway in several ways. This post walks through the full integration — from prerequisites to live data in Datasphere — and covers three ingestion approaches in detail.

One important note before we start: the delta replication approach described in this post is a proof of concept, not an official SAP recommendation. It is one way to solve the problem of incremental data synchronization, and it works — but there are other valid approaches, and your implementation may look different depending on your requirements and Datasphere expertize.

Prerequisites

This post assumes SQL View Gateway is already up and running. Specifically, you will need:

  • A provisioned, RUNNING SQL View Gateway in Cloud Portal (see the previous post)
  • A deployed role with the Commerce types you want to bring into Datasphere
  • A credential assigned to that role — this is what the Data Provisioning Agent will use to authenticate
  • An SAP Datasphere tenant

Architecture overview

The integration follows a straightforward path:

  SAP Commerce Cloud
  (SQL View Gateway)
          │ SQL / TLS 1.2+
          ▼
  SAP BTP Data Provisioning Agent (dpAgent)
          │
          ▼
  SAP Datasphere
          │
          ▼
  SAP Analytics Cloud

The Data Provisioning Agent (dpAgent) is the bridge. It is a lightweight application that you deploy and run yourself — either in a cloud environment or on a server in your internal network. It connects to SQL View Gateway using standard SQL credentials and makes the data available to SAP Datasphere as a remote source.

Where you run the dpAgent determines your connectivity model:

  • Cloud-hosted dpAgent (e.g. a VM in Azure) — connects to SQL View Gateway over the public internet; use public access mode on the gateway
  • On-premise or VPN-connected dpAgent — connects through your VPN tunnel; use VPN mode on the gateway

Either approach works. The choice depends on your network architecture and security requirements.

Step 1: Set up the Data Provisioning Agent

Setting up the dpAgent is covered in the SAP Datasphere documentation. At a high level:

  1. Create a Data Provisioning Agent entry in SAP Datasphere: go to System → Configuration → Data Integration and add a new agent. Note the agent token — you will need it during agent setup.
  2. Download and install the dpAgent on your chosen host.
  3. Start the agent and register it with your Datasphere tenant using the token from step 1.
  4. Verify the agent appears as connected in Datasphere.

Once the agent is running, two network requirements must be met:

  • The dpAgent host must be able to reach SQL View Gateway — ensure there are no firewall rules on the dpAgent host or its network that block outbound SQL traffic to the gateway endpoint.
  • SQL View Gateway must allow the dpAgent's IP — in Cloud Portal, update the gateway's allowed IPs to include the IP address from which the dpAgent will connect (its public IP if connecting over the internet, or its internal IP if connecting through VPN). This was covered in detail in the previous post.

Step 2: Create a connection in SAP Datasphere

With the dpAgent running, create a connection that points to your SQL View Gateway:

  1. In SAP Datasphere, go to Connections and create a new connection.
  2. Select Microsoft SQL Server as the connection type (SQL View Gateway exposes a standard SQL Server endpoint).
  3. Enter the connection details from Cloud Portal:
    • Host: the server name from the connection string
    • Port: as shown in Cloud Portal (typically 1433)
    • Database: the database name from the connection string
    • Username and Password: the credential you created in Cloud Portal
  4. Select the dpAgent you registered in Step 1.
  5. Test the connection and save.

If the connection test fails, work through the connectivity troubleshooting checklist from the previous post — the most common causes are allowed IP misconfiguration and DNS resolution issues.

Step 3: Choose your ingestion approach

Once the connection is established, you have three options for how to consume Commerce data in Datasphere. The right choice depends on your use case.

Option 1: Remote Tables (real-time)

Remote Tables give you a live view of Commerce data — queries run directly against SQL View Gateway on demand, with no data copied into Datasphere. This is the simplest option to set up.

In SAP Datasphere, go to Data Builder → Import Remote Tables, select your SQL View Gateway connection, and choose the views you want to expose. Datasphere creates a remote table for each view. You can use these tables directly in views, models, and stories.

Best for: dashboards that need up-to-date data, low-volume queries, prototyping.

Trade-off: every query hits SQL View Gateway. Under heavy load or complex queries, this puts pressure on the gateway — consider gateway sizing carefully, and keep in mind that a dedicated gateway per use case (as described in the previous post) isolates this load from other consumers.

Option 2: Full replication (Data Flows)

Data Flows copy Commerce data into local tables in Datasphere on a scheduled basis. After the initial load, queries run against Datasphere's own storage — no ongoing load on the gateway.

In Data Builder, create a Data Flow that reads from a remote table and writes to a local table. Schedule it to run at whatever frequency your use case requires.

Best for: bulk analytical workloads, historical trend analysis, scenarios where query performance matters more than data freshness.

Trade-off: data is only as fresh as the last scheduled run. For large tables, full reloads can be expensive — which is where delta replication helps.

Option 3: Delta replication

Delta replication copies only the records that have changed since the last run. This is significantly more efficient than full replication for large, frequently updated tables — but it requires more setup.

The approach described here uses creationtime and modifiedtime columns — which are present on every SAP Commerce type — to detect new and updated records. It is one possible implementation, not the only way to approach this problem.

One important limitation: this mechanism does not detect deleted records. A record replicated to Datasphere will remain there permanently, even if it is subsequently deleted in Commerce. Depending on your use case, this may or may not be acceptable. Handling deletions requires additional implementation on the Commerce side — for example, soft-deleting records by marking them with a flag rather than removing them from the database, and propagating that flag through the replication. The right approach depends on your requirements and is outside the scope of this post.

Setting up delta replication

How the mechanism works

The delta replication is driven by two independent clocks that work together.

The DeltaImports table acts as a synchronization manifest. Each row represents one data source to replicate. The run column holds a counter — it tracks how many times this source should have been synchronized. You control how often a source is synchronized by controlling how often this counter is incremented. A Data Flow in Datasphere handles the incrementing on a schedule you define per source.

The HANA scheduler job runs independently at a fixed interval (for example, every 10 minutes). On each run, it scans the DeltaImports table and compares each row's run value against the last recorded run in delta_import_control. If run is higher than last_run, it means at least one synchronisation is overdue — so it replicates all records created or modified since the last run, then updates last_run to match.

This two-clock design means synchronization frequency is controlled by how often you increment run, but the actual replication happens at the HANA scheduler's cadence. In practice, data will be at most one scheduler interval late relative to the run counter. For example: if a Data Flow increments run every 15 minutes, and the HANA job runs every 10 minutes, data will be synchronized approximately every 15 minutes — but never more than 10 minutes late relative to when the increment happened.

This also means you can replicate different sources at different frequencies simply by scheduling their Data Flows differently — without changing the HANA job at all.

One practical implication for sizing your scheduler interval: data will never be fresher than the HANA job schedule, regardless of how frequently you increment run. If the HANA job runs every 10 minutes, data will be at most 10 minutes stale — incrementing run every minute will not change that. Set the HANA job schedule to the maximum acceptable data latency for your use case, and treat it as the ceiling on data freshness for the entire delta replication setup.

At the same time, avoid setting the interval too aggressively. Each run of the HANA job executes queries and upsert operations against your Datasphere schema — running it every few seconds would introduce unnecessary load on the HANA side. Delta replication is designed for scheduled synchronization scenarios where data latency of minutes is acceptable, not seconds. If you need truly real-time access to Commerce data, Remote Tables are the better fit.

Overview

The mechanism works as follows:

  1. A control table in Datasphere tracks the last successful synchronization timestamp per data source.
  2. A scheduled job runs at regular intervals and upserts records that have been created or modified since the last run.
  3. A history table records each run for auditing and troubleshooting.

3.1 Create the supporting tables in SAP Datasphere

In SAP Datasphere, go to Configuration → Database Access → Database Analysis Users and create a user with Space Schema Access enabled. Open Database Explorer and connect with this user.

Creating a Database Analysis User automatically creates a corresponding Open SQL Schema — a dedicated schema in the underlying HANA database where you can create tables, views, and procedures. The schema name is derived from the username and will look something like USERNAME#SCHEMANAME. In the code snippets throughout this section, we use CCV2_DSP#COMMERCE as a placeholder — replace it with the actual schema name shown in your Database Explorer after creating the user.

In the Open SQL Schema, create the following tables:

Control table — tracks the last successful run per data source:

create column table delta_import_control (
      pk BIGINT primary key,
      last_datetime TIMESTAMP not null,
      last_run BIGINT
  );

History table — append-only log of every run:

create column table delta_import_history (
      pk BIGINT primary key generated always as identity,
      delta_import BIGINT not null,
      run_datetime TIMESTAMP not null,
      run_from TIMESTAMP,
      run_to TIMESTAMP not null,
      affected BIGINT not null,
      foreign key (delta_import) references delta_import_control
  );

3.2 Create the DeltaImports entity in Datasphere

The synchronization mechanism is driven by a table called DeltaImports that lives in your Datasphere space. It tells the system which source views to sync and where to put the results.

Create a local table called DeltaImports with the following structure (use the CSN below in Data Builder):

{
    "definitions": {
      "DeltaImports": {
        "kind": "entity",
        "elements": {
          "PK": { "type": "cds.Integer64", "key": true, "notNull": true },
          "src": { "type": "cds.String", "length": 128, "notNull": true },
          "res": { "type": "cds.String", "length": 128, "notNull": true },
          "run": { "type": "cds.Integer64", "notNull": true, "default": { "val": "0" } }
        }
      }
    }
  }
  • src — fully qualified name of the source view in Datasphere (e.g. "MYSPACE"."V_Orders")
  • res — fully qualified name of the target local table (e.g. "CCV2_DSP#COMMERCE"."Orders") — replace with your Open SQL Schema name
  • run — a counter incremented on each run; used to trigger the Data Flow

Then create a 1:1 view on DeltaImports called V_DeltaImports and mark it Exposed for analytics. This view is what the scheduled Data Flow reads.

3.3 Create the HANA procedures

Back in Database Explorer, create the following stored procedures in your Open SQL Schema.

prepare_incremental_import — called once per data source to create the target local table and grant the necessary permissions to your Datasphere space:

create or replace procedure prepare_incremental_import(
      in src NVARCHAR(128),
      in res NVARCHAR(128)
  ) as
  begin
      declare sch nvarchar(128);
      select CURRENT_SCHEMA into sch from dummy;

      exec 'create row table "' || :sch || '"."' || :res || '" as (select * from ' || :src || ')';
      exec 'alter table "' || :sch || '"."' || :res || '" add primary key ("PK")';

      CALL "DWC_GLOBAL"."GRANT_PRIVILEGE_TO_SPACE"(
          OPERATION => 'GRANT', PRIVILEGE => 'SELECT',
          SCHEMA_NAME => :sch, OBJECT_NAME => :res, SPACE_ID => 'YOUR_SPACE_ID');
      CALL "DWC_GLOBAL"."GRANT_PRIVILEGE_TO_SPACE"(
          OPERATION => 'GRANT', PRIVILEGE => 'UPDATE',
          SCHEMA_NAME => :sch, OBJECT_NAME => :res, SPACE_ID => 'YOUR_SPACE_ID');
      CALL "DWC_GLOBAL"."GRANT_PRIVILEGE_TO_SPACE"(
          OPERATION => 'GRANT', PRIVILEGE => 'DELETE',
          SCHEMA_NAME => :sch, OBJECT_NAME => :res, SPACE_ID => 'YOUR_SPACE_ID');
      CALL "DWC_GLOBAL"."GRANT_PRIVILEGE_TO_SPACE"(
          OPERATION => 'GRANT', PRIVILEGE => 'INSERT',
          SCHEMA_NAME => :sch, OBJECT_NAME => :res, SPACE_ID => 'YOUR_SPACE_ID');
  end;

Replace YOUR_SPACE_ID with your actual Datasphere space ID.

incremental_import — the core synchronization procedure. For a given source/target pair, it upserts all records modified or created since the last run:

create or replace procedure incremental_import(
      in pk bigint,
      in src NVARCHAR(128),
      in res NVARCHAR(128),
      in run bigint
  ) as
  begin
      declare dt, curr_dt timestamp;
      declare query nvarchar(2000);
      declare affected bigint;

      select UTCTOLOCAL(CURRENT_UTCTIMESTAMP, 'Etc/UTC') into curr_dt from dummy;
      select ifnull((select last_datetime from delta_import_control where pk = :pk), null)
          into dt from dummy;

      query = 'upsert ' || :res || ' SELECT * from ' || :src
          || ' where "modifiedtime" < ''' || :curr_dt || ''''
          || ' and "creationtime" < ''' || :curr_dt || '''';

      IF dt is not null THEN
          query = query || ' and ("modifiedtime" > ''' || :dt
              || ''' or "creationtime" > ''' || :dt || ''')';
      END IF;

      exec query;
      SELECT ::ROWCOUNT INTO affected from dummy;

      upsert delta_import_control(pk, last_datetime, last_run)
          values (:pk, :curr_dt, :run) WITH PRIMARY KEY;
      insert into delta_import_history(delta_import, run_datetime, run_from, run_to, affected)
          values (:pk, :curr_dt, :dt, :curr_dt, :affected);
  end;

incremental_imports — the orchestrator. Reads V_DeltaImports and calls incremental_import for each entry that has a new run value:

create or replace procedure incremental_imports as
  begin
      DECLARE pk, run BIGINT;
      DECLARE src, res NVARCHAR(128);
      DECLARE deltas table (
          pk bigint, src nvarchar(128), res nvarchar(128),
          run bigint, last_run bigint
      );
      DECLARE CURSOR c_deltas for select pk, src, res, run, last_run from :deltas;

      deltas =
          select di.pk, di."src", di."res", di."run", dic.last_run
          from "YOUR_SPACE_ID"."V_DeltaImports" di
          left outer join delta_import_control dic on di.pk = dic.pk;

      FOR delta AS c_deltas DO
          if :delta.last_run is null or :delta.run > :delta.last_run then
              call incremental_import(:delta.pk, :delta.src, :delta.res, :delta.run);
          end if;
      END FOR;
  end;

Replace YOUR_SPACE_ID with your actual space ID.

3.4 Schedule the synchronization

Create a scheduler job in HANA to run incremental_imports at your desired interval. The example below runs every 10 minutes:

CREATE SCHEDULER JOB do_incremental_imports
      CRON '* * * * * * 00,10,20,30,40,50'
      ENABLE PROCEDURE incremental_imports;

Adjust the schedule to match your freshness requirements and gateway capacity.

3.5 Register a data source for delta replication

For each Commerce type you want to replicate using this mechanism:

  1. In Data Builder, use Import Remote Tables to create a remote table for the SQL View Gateway view (e.g. salesreporting_order).
  2. Create a 1:1 view on that remote table (e.g. V_Orders) and mark it Exposed for analytics.
  3. In Database Explorer, call prepare_incremental_import to create the target table and grant permissions:
CALL prepare_incremental_import(
      '"YOUR_SPACE_ID"."V_Orders"',
      'Orders'
  );
  • Insert a row into DeltaImports to register this source:
INSERT INTO "YOUR_SPACE_ID"."DeltaImports"(PK, src, res, run)
  VALUES (
      1,
      '"YOUR_SPACE_ID"."V_Orders"',
      '"CCV2_DSP#COMMERCE"."Orders"', -- replace with your Open SQL Schema name
      0
  );

  • Create a Data Flow to trigger the synchronization.

    This Data Flow serves one specific purpose: it increments the run counter in DeltaImports for a given source. That increment is the signal that tells incremental_imports to process this source on the next scheduler run.

    The Data Flow has three nodes:

    • Source: reads from the DeltaImports local table
    • Projection: filters to the row where src matches your source view, and adds a calculated column run_inc = run + 1
    • Target: writes back to DeltaImports using upsert mode (update if exists, insert if not)

    To create it in Data Builder:

    1. Create a new Data Flow.
    2. Add DeltaImports as a source node.
    3. Add a Projection node after the source. In the projection:
      • Add a filter: "src" = '"YOUR_SPACE_ID"."V_Orders"'
      • Map all columns through unchanged (PK, src, res)
      • Add a calculated column run_inc with expression "run" + 1
    4. Add DeltaImports as a target node. Set the write mode to Upsert. Map run_inc → run, and all other columns through unchanged.
    5. Save and activate the Data Flow.

    The JSON below shows a complete example for OrderEntries — you can import it directly into Data Builder and adapt it for your source:

{
    "dataflows": {
      "DF_OrderEntries": {
        "kind": "sap.dis.dataflow",
        "contents": {
          "processes": {
            "source1": {
              "component": "com.sap.database.table.consumer",
              "metadata": {
                "label": "DeltaImports",
                "config": {
                  "service": "HANA",
                  "hanaConnection": { "configurationType": "Configuration Manager", "connectionID": "$DWC" },
                  "qualifiedName": "DeltaImports",
                  "dwcEntity": "DeltaImports"
                }
              }
            },
            "projection1": {
              "component": "com.sap.dataflow.projection",
              "metadata": {
                "label": "Projection 1",
                "config": {
                  "expression": "\"src\" = '\"YOUR_SPACE_ID\".\"V_Orders\"'",
                  "attributeMappings": [
                    { "target": "PK", "expression": "\"PK\"" },
                    { "target": "src", "expression": "\"src\"" },
                    { "target": "res", "expression": "\"res\"" },
                    { "target": "run_inc", "expression": "\"run\" + 1" }
                  ]
                }
              }
            },
            "target1": {
              "component": "com.sap.database.table.producer",
              "metadata": {
                "label": "DeltaImports",
                "config": {
                  "service": "HANA",
                  "hanaConnection": { "configurationType": "Configuration Manager", "connectionID": "$DWC" },
                  "qualifiedName": "DeltaImports",
                  "dwcEntity": "DeltaImports",
                  "mode": "append",
                  "upsert": true,
                  "attributeMappings": [
                    { "expression": "\"PK\"", "target": "PK" },
                    { "expression": "\"src\"", "target": "src" },
                    { "expression": "\"res\"", "target": "res" },
                    { "expression": "\"run_inc\"", "target": "run" }
                  ]
                }
              }
            }
          }
        }
      }
    }
  }

Replace YOUR_SPACE_ID and V_Orders with your actual space ID and source view name. Create a separate Data Flow for each Commerce type you are replicating.

Repeat steps 1–5 for each Commerce type you want to replicate.

Step 4: Model your data in Datasphere

Once your Commerce data is flowing into Datasphere — whether via Remote Tables, Data Flows, or delta replication — you can start building your data model.

The typical pattern for Commerce analytics:

  • Fact tables: transactional data — Orders, OrderEntries, ReturnRequests
  • Dimension tables: reference data — Products, Categories, Customers
  • Analytical models: join facts and dimensions into consumption views

This is standard Datasphere modeling and is covered in the SAP Datasphere documentation.

Step 5: Connect to SAP Analytics Cloud

With your data modeled in Datasphere, connecting SAP Analytics Cloud is straightforward — SAC has native connectivity to Datasphere. From there you can build stories, dashboards, and planning models on top of your Commerce data.

SAC–Datasphere integration is covered in the SAP Analytics Cloud documentation.

A few things to keep in mind

This is a proof of concept. The delta replication approach shown here is one way to solve incremental synchronization. It worked well in testing, but it is not an official SAP pattern. Your implementation may differ — for example, you might use Datasphere's built-in replication flows, a different change detection mechanism, or a third-party ETL tool. Treat the code snippets as a starting point, not a blueprint.

Data privacy and data hygiene are your responsibility. SQL View Gateway exposes raw Commerce data — it restricts access to certain internal technical types, but it does not sanitize or validate the content of the data it exposes. If your Commerce data includes values entered by end users (for example, free-text fields, addresses, or customer-supplied identifiers), treat those values as untrusted input. Validate and sanitize them before using them in downstream queries, models, or applications — SQL injection and other injection attacks are possible if user-supplied values are passed through without inspection. Separately, ensure that what you expose complies with your data processing obligations, consent requirements, and any cross-border data transfer rules applicable to your deployment.

Credential management. The dpAgent credential is a long-lived technical account. Rotate its password regularly, restrict its role to only the types needed for Datasphere, and treat the credential with the same care as any other service account.

Gateway sizing. If you use Remote Tables, every Datasphere query hits the gateway directly. Monitor gateway CPU utilization and consider a dedicated gateway for Datasphere traffic to isolate it from other consumers.

Learn more

  • SAP Datasphere documentation
  • Data Provisioning Agent documentation
  • SAP Analytics Cloud documentation
  • Business overview video
  • Configuration guide video
  • SQL View Gateway documentation

entry.title: Supporting Your SAP Data Architecture Learning Journey Through Community Discussions
entry.content:
The role of the data architect is evolving fast. It is no longer only about managing databases or designing models, it is about shaping scalable, governed, AI-ready data ecosystems that turn business strategy into real outcomes. 

SAP has recently introduced a new learning path for data architects, combining foundational architecture concepts with practical implementation topics such as data modeling, metadata, Data Mesh, Data Fabric, Data Lakehouse, SAP Business Data Cloud integration, and modern hybrid landscapes with platforms such as Snowflake, Databricks, and Google BigQuery. 

If you are following the SAP Data Architect learning journey or preparing for the certification, this community is a great place to take the next step: bring your real-world questions into practitioner discussions here. Use this thread to share what you are working through, such as: 

  • How to translate architecture principles into implementation decisions,
  • How to connect SAP Business Data Cloud with existing SAP and non-SAP landscapes, 
  • How to approach governance, metadata, ownership, and data quality in practice, 
  • How to modernize BW, analytics, or data engineering scenarios, 
  • How to prepare data foundations for AI and advanced analytics. 

The Data Professionals group is designed for hands-on practitioners, data architects, data engineers, analytics engineers, business analysts, data stewards, platform leads, SAP customers, and partners, who build, integrate, model, and operationalize data and AI solutions. So, whether you are studying for certification, applying the concepts in a project, or helping others define their data architecture roadmap: join the discussion, ask your questions, and share what you’ve learned. 

What is one real-world data architecture challenge you are trying to solve right now?

entry.title: Using Refresh Token to Consume SAC Multi-Actions Public API
entry.content:
Introduction

 

SAC Multi-Actions let you chain planning operations — such as data action,  data locking, and version management — into a single execution sequence. While these are typically triggered from the SAC UI or scheduler, many enterprise scenarios demand programmatic invocation from external systems: as part of an SAP Integration Suite flow, or from a custom microservice running on SAP BTP.
 
The SAC Multi-Actions Public API enables this, but with an important constraint: it does not support OAuth 2.0 Client Credentials. All calls must run under a business user context. There are two common patterns to achieve non-interactive authentication while preserving user identity: the OAuth 2.0 refresh token flow (covered in detail in this post), and the OAuth2 SAML Bearer Assertion flow . This blog walks through how to use refresh tokens to consume the SAC Multi-Actions Public API end-to-end.

Understanding the Authentication Challenge

Why Not Client Credentials?

The SAC Multi-Actions API requires a business user context because planning operations involve data security, audit trails, and user-specific permissions. A service principal (client credentials) cannot own planning data or appear in audit logs. Therefore, every API call must carry a user identity.

The Role of Refresh Token

In OAuth 2.0, the authorization server issues two types of tokens: access token and refresh token. While access token grant short-lived access to protected resources, refresh token are used to acquire a new access token when the current one expires — without requiring the user to re-authenticate interactively.
 
For automated integrations, this means:
  • You perform interactive authentication once to obtain the initial access token and refresh token
  • Your application stores the refresh token securely
  • When the access token expires, your application uses the refresh token to get a new access token automatically
  • The integration continues running without human intervention
     

OAuth2 Refresh Token Flow

1. Enable External API Access on Multi-Actions

In SAC, open your Multi-Actions designer, click the settings icon, and check Allow External API Access. Save the changes and note down the Starter API URL — this is your Multi-Actions execution endpoint.
 

2. Create an OAuth Client in SAC

Navigate to System > Administration > App Integration and create a new OAuth client with these settings:
  • Purpose: Interactive Usage
  • Authorization Grant: Authorization Code
  • Redirect URI: https://oauth.usebruno.com/callback which is required by Bruno
  • Token Lifetime: Access tokens are short-lived, typically valid for 1 hour
  • Refresh Token Lifetime: Refresh tokens are valid for up to 180 days, and are used to obtain new access tokens       

After saving, copy the Client ID and Client Secret (the secret is only shown once). Also note the Token URL and Authorization URL displayed on the same page.

3. Configure OAuth 2.0 in Bruno

Go to the "Auth" tab of your request collection in Bruno, and configure a new OAuth 2.0 token with the following parameters:
  • Grant Type: Authorization Code
  • Callback URL: https://oauth.usebruno.com/callback 
  • Auth URL: The Authorization URL copied from SAC
  • Access Token URL: The Token URL copied from SAC
  • Client ID: Your OAuth client ID
  • Client Secret: Your OAuth client secret        
 

 

4. Obtain the Initial Refresh Token

After configuration is done, click on "Get Access Token" at the bottom of  Bruno Auth tab.  After logging on SAC in the pop-up window, both an access_token and a refresh_token should be fetched. The step is to perform the standard Authorization Code flow, and this is a one-time interactive step. 

Important: Securely store the refresh_token — this is your long-lived credential. Never hardcode it in your application or commit it to version control.

 

5. Use Refresh Token to Get a New Access Token

When the access token expires (or proactively before expiry in production implementations), call the token endpoint with grant_type=refresh_token, along with your stored refresh token. The request uses Basic authentication with your OAuth Client ID and Client Secret.

6: Fetch CSRF Token

The access token can be used to access Multi-Actions public APIs. In addition, SAC protects write operations with CSRF (Cross-Site Request Forgery) tokens to prevent unauthorized cross-origin requests. Before sending HTTP POST requests (used for triggering Multi-Actions), you must first fetch a valid CSRF token.
 
Send a GET request to {your-tenant-url}/api/v1/csrf, including the header x-csrf-token: fetch. The CSRF token is returned in the x-csrf-token response header.
 
The CSRF token is bound to the current user session and can be reused for the lifetime of the access token. GET requests do not require a CSRF token.
  • Authorization:  Bearer <valid access token>        

 
7: Trigger Multi-Actions Execution
Use the valid access token and CSRF token in the following POST request to execute a Multi-Actions.
  • Authorization:  Bearer <valid access token>
  • Content-Type:  application/json
  • x-csrf-token:  <valid  csrf token> 

8: Get Multi-Actions Execution Result

Use the query status API to get the status of Multi-Actions execution result.

  • Authorization:  Bearer <valid access token>        

Complete Node.js Example

Below is a complete Node.js + Express example that implements the full refresh token flow, automatic CSRF token fetching, and proactive token refresh. The example uses server-side sessions for demonstration purposes; for production use, replace the in-memory session with a persistent store and a dedicated secrets manager.

Environment Configuration

Create a .env file in your project root with the following variables:

SAC_CLIENT_ID=your-oauth-client-id
SAC_CLIENT_SECRET=your-oauth-client-secret
SAC_BASE_URL=https://your-tenant-url
SAC_TOKEN_URL=https://your-tenant-token-url
SAC_AUTHORIZE_URL=https://your-tenant-authorize-url
REDIRECT_URI=http://localhost:3000/callback
PORT=3000
SESSION_SECRET=your-secure-session-secret

Full Source Code

require('dotenv').config();
const express = require('express');
const session = require('express-session');
const axios = require('axios');
const crypto = require('crypto');
const path = require('path');

const app = express();
const PORT = process.env.PORT || 3000;

// SAC OAuth 2.0 credentials
const SAC_CLIENT_ID     = process.env.SAC_CLIENT_ID;
const SAC_CLIENT_SECRET = process.env.SAC_CLIENT_SECRET;
const SAC_BASE_URL      = process.env.SAC_BASE_URL;
const SAC_TOKEN_URL     = process.env.SAC_TOKEN_URL;
const SAC_AUTHORIZE_URL = process.env.SAC_AUTHORIZE_URL;
const REDIRECT_URI      = process.env.REDIRECT_URI || `http://localhost:${PORT}/callback`;

// Refresh 60 seconds before expiry to avoid mid-request failures
const TOKEN_REFRESH_BUFFER = 60;

app.use(express.json());
app.use(express.static(path.join(__dirname, 'public')));
app.use(session({
  secret: process.env.SESSION_SECRET || 'change-me-in-production',
  resave: false,
  saveUninitialized: false,
  cookie: { secure: false, maxAge: 60 * 60 * 1000 }
}));

// ── Helper: Generate Basic Auth header ───────────────────────────────────────
function getBasicAuth() {
  return Buffer.from(`${SAC_CLIENT_ID}:${SAC_CLIENT_SECRET}`).toString('base64');
}

// ── Helper: Check if access token needs refresh ──────────────────────────────
function tokenNeedsRefresh(req) {
  if (!req.session.accessToken || !req.session.tokenExpiresAt) return true;
  return Date.now() >= req.session.tokenExpiresAt - TOKEN_REFRESH_BUFFER * 1000;
}

// ── Helper: Fetch CSRF token from SAC ────────────────────────────────────────
async function fetchCsrfToken(accessToken) {
  const response = await axios.get(`${SAC_BASE_URL}/api/v1/csrf`, {
    headers: {
      Authorization: `Bearer ${accessToken}`,
      'x-csrf-token': 'fetch'
    }
  });
  return response.headers['x-csrf-token'];
}

// ── Step 1: Redirect user to SAC authorization page ─────────────────────────
app.get('/auth/sac', (req, res) => {
  if (!SAC_CLIENT_ID || !SAC_BASE_URL) {
    return res.status(500).json({ error: 'SAC_CLIENT_ID or SAC_BASE_URL not configured' });
  }

  const state = crypto.randomBytes(16).toString('hex');
  req.session.oauthState = state;

  const params = new URLSearchParams({
    response_type: 'code',
    client_id: SAC_CLIENT_ID,
    redirect_uri: REDIRECT_URI,
    state
  });

  res.redirect(`${SAC_AUTHORIZE_URL}?${params}`);
});

// ── Step 2: SAC redirects back with authorization code ──────────────────────
app.get('/callback', async (req, res) => {
  const { code, state, error, error_description } = req.query;

  if (error) {
    return res.redirect(`/?error=${encodeURIComponent(error_description || error)}`);
  }

  if (!state || state !== req.session.oauthState) {
    return res.redirect('/?error=invalid_state');
  }
  delete req.session.oauthState;

  if (!code) {
    return res.redirect('/?error=no_code');
  }

  try {
    // Exchange authorization code for access + refresh tokens
    const tokenResponse = await axios.post(
      SAC_TOKEN_URL,
      new URLSearchParams({
        grant_type: 'authorization_code',
        code,
        redirect_uri: REDIRECT_URI
      }).toString(),
      {
        headers: {
          'Content-Type': 'application/x-www-form-urlencoded',
          Authorization: `Basic ${getBasicAuth()}`
        }
      }
    );

    const { access_token, refresh_token, token_type, expires_in, scope } = tokenResponse.data;

    req.session.accessToken     = access_token;
    req.session.refreshToken    = refresh_token || null;
    req.session.tokenType       = token_type;
    req.session.tokenExpiresAt  = Date.now() + expires_in * 1000;
    req.session.scope           = scope;

    res.redirect('/?success=true');
  } catch (err) {
    const detail = err.response?.data?.error_description || err.response?.data?.error || err.message;
    console.error('Token exchange error:', detail);
    res.redirect(`/?error=${encodeURIComponent(detail)}`);
  }
});

// ── Helper: Refresh access token ─────────────────────────────────────────────
async function refreshAccessToken(req) {
  const rt = req.session.refreshToken;
  if (!rt) throw new Error('No refresh token available');

  try {
    const tokenResponse = await axios.post(
      SAC_TOKEN_URL,
      new URLSearchParams({
        grant_type:    'refresh_token',
        refresh_token: rt
      }).toString(),
      {
        headers: {
          'Content-Type': 'application/x-www-form-urlencoded',
          Authorization:  `Basic ${getBasicAuth()}`
        }
      }
    );

    const { access_token, refresh_token, expires_in, scope } = tokenResponse.data;

    req.session.accessToken    = access_token;
    req.session.tokenExpiresAt = Date.now() + expires_in * 1000;
    req.session.scope          = scope;

    // Handle token rotation: update stored refresh token if a new one is issued
    if (refresh_token) {
      req.session.refreshToken = refresh_token;
    }

    return access_token;
  } catch (err) {
    const detail = err.response?.data?.error_description || err.response?.data?.error || err.message;
    console.error('Refresh token exchange error:', detail);
    throw new Error(`Token refresh failed: ${detail}`);
  }
}

// ── Middleware: Ensure valid access token before API calls ───────────────────
async function requireValidToken(req, res, next) {
  try {
    if (tokenNeedsRefresh(req)) {
      await refreshAccessToken(req);
    }
    next();
  } catch (err) {
    res.status(401).json({ error: err.message });
  }
}

// ── API: Get current token status ───────────────────────────────────────────
app.get('/api/token', (req, res) => {
  if (!req.session.accessToken) {
    return res.status(401).json({ authenticated: false });
  }
  const token = req.session.accessToken;
  res.json({
    authenticated: true,
    token_type:    req.session.tokenType,
    expires_in:    Math.max(0, Math.floor((req.session.tokenExpiresAt - Date.now()) / 1000)),
    scope:         req.session.scope,
    token_preview: `${token.slice(0, 6)}${'*'.repeat(6)}${token.slice(-6)}`,
    token_length:  token.length,
    has_refresh_token: !!req.session.refreshToken
  });
});

// ── API: Manually trigger token refresh ─────────────────────────────────────
app.post('/api/refresh', requireValidToken, async (req, res) => {
  try {
    await refreshAccessToken(req);
    const token = req.session.accessToken;
    const newRt = req.session.refreshToken;
    res.json({
      success: true,
      token_preview: `${token.slice(0, 6)}${'*'.repeat(6)}${token.slice(-6)}`,
      token_length:  token.length,
      expires_in:    Math.floor((req.session.tokenExpiresAt - Date.now()) / 1000),
      refresh_token_rotated: !!req.session.refreshToken,
      new_refresh_token_preview: `${newRt.slice(0, 6)}${'*'.repeat(6)}${newRt.slice(-6)}`
    });
  } catch (err) {
    res.status(500).json({ error: err.message });
  }
});

// ── API: Trigger multi-action execution ─────────────────────────────────────
app.post('/api/multiactions/:multiActionsId/executions', requireValidToken, async (req, res) => {
  try {
    // Automatically fetch CSRF token before POST request
    const csrfToken = await fetchCsrfToken(req.session.accessToken);

    const response = await axios.post(
      `${SAC_BASE_URL}/api/v1/multiActions/${encodeURIComponent(req.params.multiActionsId)}/executions/`,
      req.body,
      {
        headers: {
          Authorization: `Bearer ${req.session.accessToken}`,
          'Content-Type': 'application/json',
          'x-csrf-token': csrfToken,
          Accept: 'application/json'
        }
      }
    );
    res.status(response.status).json(response.data);
  } catch (err) {
    const status = err.response?.status || 500;
    const errorDetail = err.response?.data?.messages?.[0]?.message || err.response?.data || err.message;
    res.status(status).json({ error: errorDetail });
  }
});

// ── API: Get multi-action execution status ──────────────────────────────────
app.get('/api/multiactions/:multiActionsId/executions/:executionId', requireValidToken, async (req, res) => {
  try {
    const response = await axios.get(
      `${SAC_BASE_URL}/api/v1/multiActions/${encodeURIComponent(req.params.multiActionsId)}/executions/${encodeURIComponent(req.params.executionId)}`,
      {
        headers: {
          Authorization: `Bearer ${req.session.accessToken}`,
          Accept: 'application/json'
        }
      }
    );
    res.json(response.data);
  } catch (err) {
    const status = err.response?.status || 500;
    const errorDetail = err.response?.data?.messages?.[0]?.message || err.response?.data || err.message;
    res.status(status).json({ error: errorDetail });
  }
});

// ── API: Logout and clear session ───────────────────────────────────────────
app.post('/api/logout', (req, res) => {
  req.session.destroy(() => res.json({ success: true }));
});

app.listen(PORT, () => {
  console.log(`Server running at http://localhost:${PORT}`);
  const missing = ['SAC_CLIENT_ID', 'SAC_CLIENT_SECRET', 'SAC_BASE_URL', 'SAC_TOKEN_URL']
    .filter(k => !process.env[k]);
  if (missing.length) {
    console.warn(`WARNING: Missing environment variables: ${missing.join(', ')}. Copy .env.example to .env.`);
  }
});

Conclusion

Using refresh token to consume the SAP Analytics Cloud Multi-Actions Public API provides a robust, automated approach for integrating SAC planning operations into your enterprise workflows. By separating the initial user authentication from ongoing API access, refresh token enable long-running integrations without compromising security or requiring interactive logins.
 
The key to success lies in implementing proper token lifecycle management: secure storage of credentials, proactive refresh before access token expiry, correct handling of token rotation, and graceful degradation when the refresh token itself expires.
 
Whether you're triggering Multi-Actions orchestrating planning cycles from SAP Integration Suite, or building custom planning applications on BTP, the refresh token flow ensures your SAC integrations remain authenticated and operational around the clock.

entry.title: Why Every SAP Analytics Consultant Needs to Learn BDC in 2026 - My Personal Take
entry.content:
Over the past several years, my career has been quietly circled the same problem from different angles. I have configured sales compensation logic across multiple lines of business, implemented SuccessFactors modules, experimented with SAP BTP, written a master's thesis on low-code mobile development, earned an SAC Story Design certification and now spend my weeks living inside SAP Analytics Cloud and Power BI dashboards, with Qlik Sense in the mix too. Different projects, different tools, but really one long conversation between "business needs an answer" and "the data isn't cooperating." 

So when SAP Business Data Cloud started dominating every SAP Community feed and Sapphire keynote this year, I didn't read it as a another product launch to file away. I read it as SAP describing, almost exactly, the job I already do.

SAC Is Becoming Part of BDC And That's Not a Footnote

Here's the part that made me sit up: SAP Analytics Cloud is being folded into Business Data Cloud. SAC keeps its own storage for now, but it's gradually shifting toward BDC's storage layer, particularly Datasphere. That's not a minor backend detail for someone who builds stories and models in SAC but it's a signal that the platform I know is about to sit inside a bigger, more governed architecture and the way I design reports, connect data sources and think about "where does this number actually come from" is going to change with it.

If you're an analytics consultant and your product of choice is SAC, Power BI on top of SAP data or Qlik connected to SAP systems, you're not adjacent to this shift. You're standing right in the middle of it.

The Problem BDC Is Actually Solving Is One I have Lived

Every analytics engagement I have worked on whether it was SuccessFactors reporting, Power BI dashboards for finance or SAC stories has run into some version of the same wall: the data exists but it's scattered across systems, half-governed and everyone has a slightly different version of "the truth." You spend more time reconciling where a number came from than actually analyzing what it means.

BDC's whole premise is that this shouldn't be a per-project problem anymore. Instead of every consultant rebuilding the same data plumbing between SAP systems, Databricks, Snowflake or Big Query, SAP wants that context managed once, centrally, and trusted everywhere. When I first read that framing, it genuinely resonated and it's the exact gap I have spent years working around manually.

Why I'm Not Waiting for "Later" to Learn This

I have written SAP Community blogs before on SAP Build, on connecting BTP trial accounts to Eclipse, and more recently on hands-on impressions of the SAP Analytics Cloud Q1 2026 release. Each time, writing about a tool while I was still learning it forced me to actually understand it, not just use it on autopilot. BDC feels like the next one of those moments, except the stakes are bigger: this isn't a feature update, it's SAP redrawing the map that SAC, Datasphere and BW all sit on.

For consultants, early fluency in a shift like this tends to matter more than fluency in a shift that's already finished. Right now, most people I talk to know BDC exists but haven't gone past the keynote slide. That gap won't last SAP has already flagged that 2026 is the year BDC moves from architecture diagrams into real client pilots. I would rather be the person in the room who's actually poked at it than the person still asking what it is.

What This Means If You're Where I Am

If your background looks anything like mine SAC, Power BI, Qlik, SuccessFactors or general SAP consulting then here's my honest take on where to start:

  • Revisit your SAC projects with a BDC lens: Ask where your data actually lives today and what changes if that storage layer shifts toward Datasphere.
  • Get comfortable with the word "governance" again: BDC is as much a governance story as a technology one and that's traditionally been the less glamorous part of analytics work and which is exactly why it's undervalued and worth learning well.
  • Don't wait for a formal project to start learning: I didn't wait for a client to ask about SAP Build before writing about it. Poke around, read the architecture docs, write down your confusion because that's usually the fastest way through it.

I don't have a finished BDC implementation story to tell you yet but nobody outside a handful of pilot customers really does. What I have is a career built on exactly the problem BDC is trying to solve and enough scar tissue from reconciling data across SAP systems to know this is worth paying attention to early, not after it's already the standard.

If you're an analytics consultant reading this and thinking "I'll get to BDC eventually"  then I'd gently push back. The people who get to it first are the ones who get to shape how their clients adopt it, instead of just reacting to it.

 

I would love to hear from others in the SAP Community , if you've started exploring BDC in your SAC or Datasphere work, what's actually surprised you so far?

Drop it in the comments, I'm collecting real experiences, not just keynote takeaways.😊

entry.title: SAP Business Data Cloud - New Search and Replace actions in filter token
entry.content:
Introduction

When filtering in SAP Analytics Cloud, you're usually doing one of two things: quickly finding a set of members and applying them immediately or refining your selection step by step before committing. The redesigned search experience in the Q3 2026 release is built around both - using the new Replace and Update actions.

New Search and Replace: Get to Your Filter Selection Faster

There is no need to manually deselecting "All" before you start. When you enter a search term, the previous selection is no longer in the way.

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

During an active search, the “Apply Selection” button discards the previous selected members and replace them with the current search result directly.

For more complex selections, run multiple consecutive searches to build up your final list before committing:

  • Replace starts fresh: the current selection is completely discarded and replaced with the search result. Members that were previously selected but are not in the search results will be removed.
  • Update adds to what you have: Search results are merged into the existing selection. Previously selected members are kept, and any search results are added on top. Any deselections made during the search are also applied.
  • “Apply Selection" commits the draft and closes the search.

 

 

Whether you are filtering with a single search or composing a list across multiple searches: all search results are pre-selected. Users can confirm immediately or refine from there, without having to manually select each member first.

Availability

This feature is planned for SAP Analytics Cloud Q3.2026 QRC and is expected to be available across all major filter contexts:

  • Story Filter
  • Page Filter
  • Widget Filter

entry.title: Certified as an SAP Data Architect? Join the practitioner discussion
entry.content:
Congratulations to everyone who has completed or is working toward the new SAP Data Architect certification. Becoming certified is an important milestone. It shows that you are building the knowledge needed to design scalable, governed, AI-ready data ecosystems and connect architecture concepts to business outcomes. 

But certification is also just the beginning. Once you start applying these concepts in real projects, new questions naturally come up: 

  • How do I translate architecture principles into implementation decisions? 
  • How should I approach governance, metadata, ownership, and data quality? 
  • How do I connect SAP Business Data Cloud with existing SAP and non-SAP landscapes? 
  • How do data architecture choices change when AI, advanced analytics, or data products are involved? 
  • What patterns are other practitioners using in the field? 

That is where the Data Professionals group comes in. The Data and Analytics Learning Group is the right place to learn, prepare, and ask questions about learning content. The Data Professionals group is the next step: a practitioner community for discussing real-world architecture, governance, integration, analytics, AI, and SAP Business Data Cloud scenarios with peers, SAP experts, customers, and partners. 

If you have completed the certification, are preparing for it, or are applying the learning journey in your current role, we invite you to join the practitioner discussion here. Bring your project questions, your lessons learned, your architecture challenges, and your practical experience. 

Why don’t you start with one real-world data architecture question you would like to discuss with other practitioners?

entry.title: Working with BDC in the Autonomous Enterprise: The Enterprise Architect Perspective
entry.content:
AI will not replace enterprise architecture. It will depend on it.

For enterprise architects working with SAP Business Data Cloud (BDC), that single idea reframes the Autonomous Enterprise. Your mandate hasn’t changed. You still ensure the structural integrity of the enterprise data and application landscape through governed architectures, trusted semantics, and scalable integration. What has changed is who, and what, your architecture now serves.

With the SAP Business AI Platform (BAIP), AI agents join humans and applications as first-class consumers of the enterprise landscape. Your architecture becomes the semantic contract that binds all three to a shared source of truth.

 

What stays the sam

Your core responsibilities are intact:

  • Designing end-to-end data flows across SAP Datasphere, SAP BW, SAP Analytics Cloud, SAP HANA Cloud, SAP Master Data Governance, and SAP Reltio.
  • Defining integration patterns across SAP and non-SAP systems.
  • Establishing standards for identity, security, lineage, lifecycle, scalability, resilience, cost, and compliance.

None of this is sidelined. In fact, it matters more, because AI now depends on it.

 

What changes in practice

The biggest shift: AI agents are now architectural consumers, not just applications or users.

In the Autonomous Enterprise, AI agents reason over governed BDC data products rather than loosely curated datasets. That means every architectural decision how data products are modeled, how integrations are designed, how governance is enforced, directly shapes how trustworthy, safe, and scalable AI-driven decisions will be.

Every well-designed data product and reusable integration pattern now serves both human and AI consumption, dramatically reducing the risk of shadow AI built on ungoverned data.

 

How AI-integrated architecture works with BDC

AI-driven execution does not bypass your architectural blueprints. It builds on them.

BDC empowers AI agents across the enterprise by serving as the AI knowledge core. Data products defined in BDC, sourced from SAP applications, BW objects, or Datasphere models. They carry business meaning, lineage, and access rules. BAIP uses these data products as its trusted context layer for AI reasoning and action. When AI agents generate recommendations, trigger workflows, or execute processes, they do so against this governed semantic layer and are designed to inherit the same authorizations and policies you defined.

For enterprise architects, this means designing architectures that expose data products as reusable, governed, AI-consumable assets, not just as feeds into dashboards or reports.

A simple example: a governed revenue KPI data product defined once in SAP Datasphere can be consumed by a planner in SAP Analytics Cloud, a Fiori app, and a BAIP agent that triggers an approval workflow, all using the same semantics and authorizations.

 

Trust, governance, and risk

One of the biggest enterprise AI challenges is trust: How do you ensure AI operates within the same boundaries as human users and applications?

In the Autonomous Enterprise architecture, trust is enforced by design. Because AI agents rely on the same BDC-managed semantics, master data, and access policies used by human consumers. AI outputs are designed to reflect the same KPI logic, hierarchies, filters, and authorization rules. Governance applies consistently whether the consumer is a dashboard, a planner, a business application, or an AI agent.

The result: fewer parallel governance frameworks for AI and compliance, auditability, and risk controls that extend naturally into the AI layer, keeping the architecture defensible under regulatory scrutiny.

 

How does this map to BDC components

For enterprise architects, the strategic component mapping is clear.

  • SAP Datasphere is the semantic backbone of the Autonomous Enterprise, the primary place where governed data products, semantic models, and business context are defined and exposed, providing authoritative definitions for process-critical KPIs and data domains.
  • SAP Analytics Cloud remains the primary consumption layer for analysis, planning, and AI-assisted insight generation.
  • SAP Master Data Governance ensures master data consistency underpins analytical, transactional, and AI-driven outcomes.
  • SAP Reltio delivers an AI-ready, context-rich, trusted data foundation harmonizing across SAP and non-SAP landscapes.
  • SAP HANA Cloud provides a high-performance data foundation that supports advanced analytical queries, vector processing, and low-latency access for applications and AI agents.
  • The embedded Databricks partnership, complemented by Snowflake interoperability, extends BDC with open lakehouse capabilities, enabling AI/ML workloads and unstructured data processing via zero-copy Delta Sharing.
  • The SAP Business AI Platform (BAIP) uses these components as its trusted context layer, enabling AI agents to reason and act on governed enterprise data.

Together, these components form the trusted architectural foundation on which the Autonomous Enterprise runs.

 

What to focus on now

As an enterprise architect, the most valuable preparation is not evaluating new AI tools. It is strengthening the foundation AI will rely on. Concretely, invest in:

  • Well-designed data products with clear ownership, lineage, and reusability.
  • Consistent semantics across SAP and non-SAP domains.
  • Federated governance models that scale across business units.
  • Integration patterns that treat data products as reusable APIs, not point-to-point pipelines.
  • Identity, access, and policy frameworks that extend seamlessly to AI agents.
  • Openness and interoperability leveraging Delta Sharing and open formats to future-proof your architecture.

The stronger your foundation today, the more reliable and trustworthy your AI-driven decisions will be tomorrow.

 

The takeaway

  • The Autonomous Enterprise does not replace enterprise architecture with AI. It makes enterprise architecture the governed foundation on which trustworthy AI depends.
  • The architectures you design, govern, and evolve no longer serve only people and applications. They shape how AI itself reasons and acts across the enterprise.

 

Continue the Conversation

Join the Enterprise Architecture community to share patterns, discuss AI-integrated designs, and see how peers are evolving their BDC architectures.

 

Useful resources:

  • SAP Architecture Center
  • SAP AI-native North Star architecture
  • SAP Business AI Platform
  • SAP Business Data Cloud
  • Blog: A pragmatic roadmap to an autonomous enterprise
  • SAP Learning: Guiding AI-Driven Transformation as an SAP Enterprise Architect
  • SAP Learning: The Role of Enterprise Architects in Driving the Autonomous Enterprise
